context.description: The text explains an exercise that expands awareness from point-focused attention across the whole visual field and then toward the inner world. It describes the difference between attention and awareness, along with inner inertia, resistance, and the frustration that arises when maintaining the state becomes difficult.
normalization_notes: Translated German prose into English summary text.
Mapped source spirituality-like reflection to consciousness and mind taxonomy.

rows.paragraph_text: Awareness can shift attention almost instantly between distant points in the visual field, then expand from point attention to awareness of the whole field.
rows.text_content: Awareness can shift attention almost instantly between distant points in the visual field, then expand from point attention to awareness of the whole field.

rows.paragraph_text: The passage describes inner inertia that pulls awareness back into point attention and makes sustained practice effortful.
rows.text_content: The passage describes inner inertia that pulls awareness back into point attention and makes sustained practice effortful.

rows.paragraph_text: It then introduces extending awareness into the inner world as a next step toward dual-track awareness, while noting the resistance and frustration that can interrupt the exercise.
rows.text_content: It then introduces extending awareness into the inner world as a next step toward dual-track awareness, while noting the resistance and frustration that can interrupt the exercise.

content.free_text: book_page consciousness_and_mind philosophy_of_consciousness Book page on consciousness and awareness practice. Topics include point attention, whole-field awareness, inner inertia, resistance, and dual-track awareness.
